Shortly after I took up meditation some years ago, I started experiencing a set of physical and psychological symptoms that made it difficult to continue the practice, so I dropped it for a while to see if they would go away. From then on I would make repeated attempts at picking it back up. At first they would go decently, but after a while the symptoms would return.

Over time the symptoms spread to other areas, usually those involving an attempt at self-improvement, such as practicing the SoP or studying for school. Even so, there were brief moments of what I could only describe as a feeling of wholeness during which those symptoms disappeared. I eventually decided to focus my practices on finding out how to reach that state.

Long story short, after two years of practice, this past week I was finally able to figure out the cause of my symptoms. The closest terms for it that I've seen are heart-mind split, soul-loss, or a failure to integrate the anima. Now that I've figured out the cause and begun trying to heal the split, I've noticed a huge improvement in just about every area of my life, from the emotional to the professional.

A lot of people have said that this problem is particularly common in the western world. If so, I think it would help explain the reason why so many westerners ascribe to hard determinism. My symptoms progressed cyclically, and whenever they hit their peak, they were often accompanied by a feeling of helplessness as the mind tried and failed to make the body comply with its goals. Since I identified with the mind, it felt as if something other than me was moving the body. These episodes were always followed by a short-lived period of greater integration as I intuited that the thing moving my body around should also be understood as a part of me. I was able to do this thanks to my familiarity with occult theories and related works, such as those of Jung, and the occasional glipses of a greater wholeness letting me know there was something beyond this condition. Most people who suffer from the condition aren't aware of these things, so when they feel this helplessness, they take it as an inherent part of life and not the result of a pathological mental state.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
My condition first manifested as some sort of energy being at work in the area of my head, producing uncomfortable sensations like feeling something pushing against my skull and causing my jaws to clench whenever I tried meditating. Over time this became less severe, but other symptoms developed. Physically, I began to experience fasciculations—sudden bouts of twitching localized to one small group of muscle fibers at a time, which could happen in any one of several parts of my body for no apparent reason. Psychologically, whenever the condition hit its peak there was, like I said, the feeling that something else was moving the body, and that I had no say in what it did. There was also a loss of motivation as I ceased to find meaning in anything and could find no reason why I should choose to do any one thing over any other. Whenever I tried to address these symptoms, there was often a terrible sense of frustration born from knowing I was doing something wrong but having no clue what that was.

At first the only way I had of dealing with the condition was to immerse myself in distractions, but I never remained satisfied with that for long, and once the symptoms had disappeared for a while I would feel comfortable enough to have another go at figuring how to reach that elusive state of wholeness. As I became more familiar with the workings of my psyche, there began to appear another phase in the cycle, one marked by a greater degree of integration. I would happen upon some practice which would heal the split for a moment, but until this last week it always proved to be a temporary thing, lasting a day or two at most. As I felt that ephemeral state slipping away form me I would use that technique which had allowed me to reach it in the first place to try and get it back, but it never worked. I would then grow frustrated and once again immerse myself in distractions for a while.

Through all this the only practice I kept up somewhat-constantly was the observation of what was going on in my psyche . Not as in what I was thinking and feeling, but as in how those thoughts and feelings behaved, how they affected each other, what areas of my psyche were responsible for which mental functions, things like that. Like a baby gradually learning how to move their body, I gradually learned to differentiate different parts of the psyche from each other.

I learned that all those psychological symptoms I was experiencing arose from a very specific part of my psyche—the part I understood to be "me", and which could be called the mind. I realized there was another part of my psyche, one which did not feel the frustration that "I" felt, and which kept my body doing stuff even when "I" felt there was no purpose in anything.

Eventually, after a great deal of trial and error and much reflecting on my experiences, it all clicked together for me and I figured out how to "be" this other part of me, and not just my mind. It was only afterwards that I realized this other part of my psyche is reminiscent of descriptions I've read about things like the heart, soul, or anima, and from there drew the connection with the various pathologies various traditions have described in regards to those.

As for books, most of what I've had only touches on the topic tangentially and doesn't go in-depth on it, so I can't recommend much. I can't really say how much reading them helped me out since it feels less like they helped me understand my condition and more like understanding my condition helped me understand what they had been trying to tell me all along. Still, if you're interested in it, you can't go wrong with Jung. I don't know if he had any book dealing specfically with the anima, but you might try looking for one. Alternatively, do an online search for terms like soul-loss, heart-mind spit, and anima integration to see if any of those conditions resonates with whatever you're experienceing.

Would you mind elaborating further on these symptoms and your experiences? This cyclical dynamic sounds a little bit like what I've experienced over the past five years since taking up ceremonial magic. On the whole it has improved things for me greatly, but there is something not quite coming together.

I have gone through this cycle a number of times:
- I have a dream leaving me with a powerful impression that I need to make a particular practice important in my life, and that it will fulfill me and provide the overarching meaning in my life that I crave
- I throw myself into it (within the limits of having a full time job and family), sustaining serious practice for up to 6 months
- I hit a brick wall of commitments, illness or other chaos, and the energy driving the practice, and the sense of certainty about pursuing it fizzle out
- I feel lost again.

There is another cycle applying to my job, and I find myself periodically checking the job ads for a month or so driven by feelings of inadequacy and lost potential, then entering a period of acceptance and partial re-engagement.

I should note I don't have this cycle with my magical practice itself which has been rock solid for five years, and which I have maintained through hellish difficulties. There is something funny going on beyond the usual human failings. It feels like a rocket trying to launch but losing power over and over again as the count gets to 0.

I've also recently found some of Alan Watts' lectures and while I am wary of applying his ideas willy nilly while I'm studying a different path, thinking about things like the backwards law, wu wei and complete acceptance of what is beyond my control has allowed me to regain some level of power over the sense of helplessness and futility I periodically feel about the course of my life.

I can continue on broken like this for the rest of my life if need be, but I would like to solve this, because I would prefer that the much greater person I might be exists and gets to do their thing. Any further information you could provide would be greatly appreciated!

If that helps shed light on the issue, I'd like to share my story about a similar condition, one that psychology describes as the "hidden schizoid" type of personality disorder. It began in elementary school, I believe, when normal social experiences proved too impactful to my sensitive psyche (I'm very much an artistic guy). The effect in practice was that my mind separated from the empathic part of the psyche, basically a brain/heart split, and thus I had to navigate the world of human interactions in the way a foreigner has to figure out the language and culture of a strange land, because the only tool left for me was rational analysis.

After much unpleasantness with regards to relationships (which hardly worked at all), one especially dramatic breakup triggered the collapse of this personality structure that had developed over about 25 years. That was a really painful process, but it allowed the "discontinued" parts of my psyche to finally have a say again.

In the years since then, the most effective approach I've tried to help heal this mess has been working through our host's Learning Ritual Magic. The daily banishing, Middle Pillar Exercise and meditations have proven supremely helpful, and it's an interesting little factoid that when I first familiarized myself with the tarot by drawing one random card per day and meditating on it, it was the Empress almost every time for two weeks straight, and the High Priestess almost every other time.

Thanks for the bit about sometimes not feeling as if it's yourself moving the body! I have heard that described as de-personalisation and it seems psychology has very little to say about it. Your explanation made more sense about it than anything I've heard about it so far, and I hope I can put it to practice, because boy, does it suck to experience your own life from a third person's perspective!

Finally, I'm sure this field of experiences is wide-spread in the west. Our civilization is so machine-like, and demands from its inmates to be like machines themselves, that this kind of self-sabotage might be the easiest way for many people to fit in.

I gather the Behennian star Capella is good for toothache, according to Agrippa; but it seems optimistic to suppose there will be a decent election for that star in the short time remaining, by the rather demanding rules of electional astrology, which I have by no means mastered in any case. I've been doing these fairly regularly for several years. In the past I've done, or attempted, full on rituals like the Formula of the Equinox, though I found it difficult to remember and perform all the elements of the ritual without fluffing. Also, I might be able to manage a talisman of Capella if a suitable election presented itself. In the past I carefully copied down Chris Warnock's instructions for various types of elections - planetary, fixed stars, and mansions of the Moon - that he set forth from time to time on his online forum Spiritus Mundi, and am pretty sure I can dig these out if needed. I have a symbolic Hieronymus machine that I made by hand with considerable labor using India ink on high quality material. And that's about it.

replies.title: Re: Coping with pain & anticipation thereof

I've had five root canals. You won't feel any pain, except for the initial numbing. What you will feel is anxiety over having someone's hands and face in your mouth for hours (my longest one was 3 hours for a molar in the back). It's incredibly tedious work if the doctor spends the time to do it right. You want him/her to do it right so there is no root left to grow back. Otherwise the pain will return and you'll have to do it again or pull the tooth.

Don't try to relax. Explore the emotions you are feeling in real time. Breathe.

replies.title: Re: Coping with pain & anticipation thereof
replies.content:
I was in your position before my first root canal. I especially do not do well with tooth pain, and the local anesthetics don't work well with me. I've had a lot of pain just getting a simple filling.

I have no magic suggestions, but I have now had four of those root canals. For me, there's usually some pain during the procedure, but it's not been that bad. Be sure to tell your endodontist your concerns about pain sensitivity before the procedure. There's a lot they can do. I also try to focus on relaxing all my jaw muscles as much as possible. This seems to help the anesthetic get into the tooth. Maybe it improves the blood flow?

As for after the procedure, I had one root canal where I did have a lot of pain afterwards. It was my own fault. I started chewing on the tooth too soon. I'd advise being very careful about that!

replies.title: Re: Coping with pain & anticipation thereof
replies.content: I've had a couple of root canals and it doesn't hurt that much. In fact it's kind of satisfying. It feels like scratching a really deep itch (tooth extraction can feel like that too). What I would suggest though is to practice swallowing with your mouth open. It'll be open for a while and there can be a sort of 'waterboarded by your own saliva' sensation. But if you can comfortably swallow with an open mouth, you can control it and it's fine. A few days or a week after one root canal I had a really nasty ache deep in the jaw that wasn't going away on its own. However a Doxy vibrator on the highest setting and pressed against the underside of the jaw solved that immediately. I found the teeth being prepared for the crowns more unpleasant because the drilling goes on for so long, but haven't figured out anything to improve that.

comments.title: Body of Light vs Halls of the Hollow Forms
comments.content:
I picked up Mark Stavish's Between the Gates several years ago, with the intention of working through it or a modified version of it once I'd finished the Dolmen Arch. I've read the book several times, and noticed that a passage from the Dolmen Arch bears close resemblance to the one of the primary aims of Between the Gates, but differs sharply in tone.

One of the disciplines Between the Gates teaches is the body of light, known in other traditions as the diamond body, rainbow body, or resurrection body. The body of light is an astral construct infused with one's own life force, which may attain it's own rudimentary and independent consciousness (Stavish unequivocally warns against this). Once created, the body of light is "absorbed" into the physical body, in which state it brings increased health, vitality, and magical power by merging with one's etheric body. The mage can also project the body of light from the physical body and work magic on it, so that when it is reabsorbed the mage gains the benefits of the magic. The body of light can also be assumed the same way one would assume a godform, and adopted the same way one would adopt an animal form. Stavish states that the body of light can be a method of life extension, and also that the body of light can serve as an after-death vehicle. Stavish implies this last is a worthy goal which would help advance the magician's spiritual development, turning one's death into a conscious, magical act.

The relevant passage from the Dolmen Arch is from Grade IV, Triad V (page 109): ". . . It is possible for an initiate to develop his astral shell to such a degree of solidity and resistance that it continues to hold the higher principles of the self within itself after the death of the physical body. . . [This] represents a particular perversion of the path of initiation - the use of certain highly specialized disciplines to create an astral form that will endure after physical death." The passage then goes on to describe the unfortunate fate of those who pursue the act, and describes them basically as you describe vampires in your book Monsters.

Stavish is not shy with warnings about potential pitfalls in his books, but he never mentions anything like the description form the Dolmen Arch in regard to the body of light.

Is this a case of one act being done for very different motives? Are they even the same act? As I've mentioned, I would like to work through the curriculum of Between the Gates, but the passage from the Dolmen Arch gives me pause. Do you have any advice?

Thanks

replies.title: Re: Body of Light vs Halls of the Hollow Forms
replies.content: Mark is a very capable occultist, and he comes from a tradition that differs in important ways from mine. If you find the approach taught in the Dolmen Arch congenial, you may have to modify certain aspects of Mark's curriculum -- for example, anything that would tend to make the body of light permanent may be best left alone. Divination and meditation are useful tools here.

replies.title: Re: Body of Light vs Halls of the Hollow Forms
replies.content:
Does the potential danger lie in mixing the two systems? And if so, would leaving off my DA practices and doing the Between the Gates curriculum exclusively for the time I do it help?

Is it a matter of my being enmeshed with the egregor of the Dolmen Arch, and of that egregor viewing the body of light a certain way?

I guess what I'm trying to understand is the potential mechanism of the danger, so that I can better know how to avoid it if possible.

replies.title: Re: Body of Light vs Halls of the Hollow Forms
replies.content: No, the danger isn't in mixing systems. It's purely a question of which system is correct about the postmortem consequences of that specific practice.

replies.title: Re: Body of Light vs Halls of the Hollow Forms
replies.content:
Ah, okay. I realize I had been assuming as a matter of course that both were right. It actually makes a great deal of sense that they are both conjectural, though, since it's a practice that by it's very nature is difficult to collect outcome data on.

Thanks as always!

replies.title: Re: Body of Light vs Halls of the Hollow Forms
replies.content: While working through circles/paths, would it be suitable to work through between the gates as well. I'm currently practicing (not actually doing so to speak) lucid dreaming and circles/paths with no problem.

replies.title: Re: Body of Light vs Halls of the Hollow Forms
replies.content:
"anything that would tend to make the body of light permanent may be best left alone."

This spooked me.

Is there a general way to tell whether a practice is crystalizing the aura? I've heard the Circulation of Light does that, but does the middle pillar? Does any kind of internal energy work have this effect?

I've typically heard of the crystallization of the aura as a good thing, but I wouldn't want to vampirize myself. Is vampirism the result of crystalizing the aura without integrating the lower genius?

More generally: at what level of theurgic attainment is it more beneficial to crystalize the aura than to simply let it disperse and reincarnate?

replies.title: Re: Body of Light vs Halls of the Hollow Forms
replies.content: Most practices don't crystallize the aura, and the Middle Pillar in particular does not -- instead, it fills the aura with energies from the macrocosm, which has the opposite effect. The problem is exactly one of crystallizing the aura around the lower self, rather than opening it to the higher self, at which point it becomes not crystallized but radiant, and self-sustaining not by rigidity and closedness, but by free participation in the constant creative flow of divine life.

replies.title: Re: Body of Light vs Halls of the Hollow Forms
replies.content: In what important ways, if I may ask? I have started working with Between the Gates as well, but I am more familiar with your works. Thanks.

replies.content: It's been a while since I read it. I'd suggest reading it closely and then comparing what it says with what my books say -- then make up your own mind about who's correct.

replies.title: Re: Vipassana
replies.content:
Have you cleared your browser's cookies? I can read the article unpaywalled and I'm not a subscriber (but do clear cookies several times a day).

As someone who also had some intense experiences after taking up western Buddhist meditation over ten years ago (I wouldn't necessarily call them bad ones, but they did change a lot) I thought this was excellent. The article is particularly focused on how longer periods of meditation, whether that means over half an hour, or long retreats with days full of meditation, may precipitate mental breakdowns even in some people with little to no history of mental health issues.

This paragraph - I think some of which was quoted on the other blog - helped me make sense of what I experienced in a new way, and helps contextualise the practice as a whole.

“These contemplative practices were invented for monastics who had renounced possessions, social position, wealth, family, comfort, and work,” writes David McMahan, a professor of religious studies at Franklin and Marshall College, in a 2017 book, Meditation, Buddhism, and Science. Monks and nuns sought to transcend the world and its cycles of rebirth and awaken in nirvana, an unfathomable state of equanimity beyond space and time, or at least avoid being reincarnated as a mountain goat or a hungry spirit in the hell realm underground. In the Pali suttas, the earliest Buddhist texts, the Buddha discusses meditation almost exclusively with audiences of followers ready to reject all earthly belongings. “Generally meditation is presented as something monastics aspiring to full awakening do,” McMahan writes, “an activity that is part of a way of being in the world that is ultimately aimed at exiting the world, rather than a means to a happier, more fulfilling life within it.”

Meditation can then perhaps be linked to the decluttering trend which began a couple of years afterwards. It's intriguing what the long term effects might be from its popularity with people who didn't have problems from it exactly, but may have been pointed in an unexpected direction

Sir and Druid,
A few astrological questions:
What instruments are there for observational astrology? I recall anecdotes of the old Arab astrologers observing the night sky, and coming up with accurate horoscopes that aided them in the circumstances they were in. I am aware of astrolabes, but is there such a thing as an astrological sextant?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Both these questions are way outside my knowledge. I've read that astrologers back in the day used a variety of instruments to track the heavens -- especially important were devices to measure the exact altitude of sun, moon, or first-magnitude stars above the horizon, which gave a precise measure of time -- but I don't know anything about the instruments they used. As for slide rule design, that requires a mastery of mathematics I don't have -- I can use a slide rule, but don't ask me to construct one!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
JMD is correct about astrolabes (and similar), which are used to measure the angle of celestial bodies above the horizon; and by measuring a fixed star, one could indirectly measure time. They are not calculators. A sextant could also be used this way (they are relatively modern).

Astrologers before Kepler & Newton would have used the Ptolemy’s Almagest, a 13-volume set of mathematical methods that gives fair accuracy. But it is not simple to use. You can still obtain the Almagest; an abridged one is here:

https://www.alibris.com/Almagest-Introduction-to-the-Mathematics-of-the-Heavens-Claudius-Ptolemy/book/29064689?matches=5

**************

You can use a standard slide rule to calculate logarithms and basic trig functions. You can also use printed tables to obtain these values, which are more accurate.

But astrology requires some serious celestial mechanics involving spherical trigonometry. Being able to perform simple log and trig functions is hardly the beginning of the work.

You might start with:
There is ‘Practical Astronomy With Your Calculator’, 1983,
https://www.alibris.com/Practical-Astronomy-with-Your-Calculator-Peter-J-Duffett-Smith/book/28336874?matches=17,

which is explicitly derived from a named classic celestial mechanics/spherical trig textbook (which I have, but can’t recall the title of offhand). The following is likely equivalent:

https://www.alibris.com/booksearch?mtype=B&keyword=Celestial+Calculations%3A+A+Gentle+Introduction+to+Computational+Astronomy&hs.x=30&hs.y=20


But a slide rule for celestial mechanics? Well, consider that you need
A precise time (i.e. combined date and clock-time represented as a single number)
Latitude
Longitude

The calculator output (for a specified planet) would be
right ascension
declination
-or equivalent

When you consider that a regular slide rule has one input and one output, and that an astrological slide rule would require 3 inputs and produce 2 outputs, you can well imagine it would have to be very complex. I have my doubts a practical design is possible. BUT the antikythera mechanism was quite likely an analog computer that does exactly what you want, albeit with Ptolemy’s mathematics. That would make a fun, albeit challenging, project. It would be even more challenging to redesign it implementing modern celestial mechanics!

In the meantime, you might wish to rely on a printed ephemeris.

replies.title: Clarification
replies.content:
Gah! Sorry for the misleading wording:

"A sextant could also be used this way", *this way* referring to using the sextant to measure angles just as you would with an astroloabe.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hello again. The textbook I have in mind is ‘Textbook on Spherical Astronomy’ by W.M. Smart. I have the 6th Ed (1977), and any earlier should be fine. Later editions assume you have a computer.

After my comment above, I just couldn’t stop thinking about your question. And now, I’m thinking the problem might not be quite as complicated as I had made it out to be; but first, I have several questions for JMG:
1) Does latitude have have any effect on the chart? (birth, mundane etc…). I’m guessing it doesn’t.
2) The distance of the planet above or below the ecliptic does not factor in interpreting the chart, no?

So if those questions are “No’s”, then the inputs become:
1) Date + clock-time
2) Longitude

And the output becomes:
Right ascension.

So the calculations should become significantly if not yuugely simplified compared to what I was thinking above.

Now I have a third question for you JMG: That being the case, I can’t be the first one to have noticed. Which means that surely modern astrologers before the age of computers must have known of simplified calculations. So JMG, did those astrologers use such? Or did they just rely on the ephemeris?

Whatever those equations are or must be, they may lend themselves to graphical solution; a slide-rule as such can’t work because it only has one degree of freedom (moves in one only dimension). An astrological calculator will have to have to have two degrees of freedom, so a graphical approach, perhaps a nomograph of some sort, might enable some pretty handy chart casting without a computer or ephemeris.

replies.title: Constructing a slide-rule and such
replies.content:
"Graphical and Mechanical Computation", by Joseph Lipka (MIT), copyright 1918, will tell you a lot about creating specialized slide rules, nomographs, even graphs that express relations between up to four variables.

For four-variable relations, use a cross-hair 2-D slide (3 degrees of freedom), connecting scales on opposite edges of the page by sliding and rotating one line, then sliding the rotated cross-hair such that the cross-line lands on a 3rd scale (while still connecting the first two, as before), and then the desired quantity is found at the intersection of the fourth scale and the cross-line! However, the accuracy of the answer depends on the scaling of the axes, probably between 1% and 0.1% of the range.

Lathechuck

comments.title: Magical methods to make my father less of a presence in my life?
comments.content:
I have a very tangled and dysfunctional relationship with my father, who still insists on being very present in my life even if I'm well into adulthood. While he was far from an ideal parent when I was growing up, he's never been physically abusive (verbally and emotionally might be a different story, though) or done anything else that would justify cutting off all contact, but if it were up to me I'd still rather not have him in my life at all.

Is there any magical method I could use to make him less interested in me and my life and more focused on other pursuits? Even if he he has a lot of projects and is the type who always has to be doing something, I suspect his own life is rather emotionally barren, which is one reason he "parasitizes" those around him (not just me, it's a long story). I always feel thoroughly exhausted and "drained" after spending time with him, even more than I usually do in social situations as an introvert.

I'm aware of the raspberry jam principle, and I don't want to harm him in any way, just make him less inclined to talk to me, think about me, want to help me or visit me. I've moved a few hours away from my hometown, partly but not only to establish more distance towards my father, and it's helped to an extent, but only to an extent. I'll also acknowledge my father is rather wealthy and that I've benefited in many ways from this, but I'd be willing to give up those privileges in a heartbeat if it meant seeing much less of him.

In terms of experience, I've been doing the Heathen LBRP plus MP for a while, but other than that I'm very much a novice. I know this might be more of a long-term thing, but just curious what options there might be as I hopefully proceed with my GD practice.

Thank you for your time and for providing this forum!

replies.title: Re: Magical methods to make my father less of a presence in my life?
replies.content: First, any time you know you're going to encounter him, wear a protective amulet -- the one described in the Magic Monday FAQ is good for this -- and take a hoodoo bath after it's over. Second, choose a Heathen deity who is associated with silence and isolation, and make appropriate offerings to him or her, asking for help in putting some distance between yourself and an overbearing father.

comments.title: Translation of SoP or not?
comments.content:

replies.title: Re: Translation of SoP or not?
replies.content: You should certainly translate it. The saying is quite specific: "Change not the barbarous names of evocation, for they have in the holy rites a power ineffable." What that means, translated out of Thomas Taylor's flowery prose, is that if you have words of power, divine names, or mantras that get their power from specific sonic effects, don't change them; on the other hand, if what's going on has to do with the meaning of an incantation, as is true of the SoP, put that in your native language so that it communicates well to all levels of your mind.

My parents divorced when I was 4 years old, under circumstances which have remained mysterious to this day. I am now 38.

This weekend I was able to wrangle a bit more information from my mother. Here are the facts of the case.  

It seems that, following a year of heavy drug use including cocaine and hallucinogens, my father suffered a nervous breakdown and was forcibly committed to a mental hospital by police. When my mother finally found him and went to visit, she found that his personality had utterly altered. His manner of bearing, habit of speech, and style of dress had changed utterly, so that he seemed a different person. She describes once having to be alone with him in an elevator in the hospital, and feeling utterly afraid for reasons that she wasn't able to describe. She also said that, at the hospital, he had convinced the doctors and staff that she was the drug addict, not him, and had forced him to take drugs with her. If you knew my mother, this is so laughably false that it's hard to imagine anyone even thinking it-- and yet he'd somehow convinced the hospital staff of it.

His old personality never returned. He left and started a new family. When I was young he would visit occasionally. My mother tells me that he would regularly corner her when no one was around and that he had a kind of "slimy, used car salesman vibe, but not exactly that." I have very little memory of his visits. I do remember, though, that an arrangement had been made with my elementary school teachers that the day after my father visited, no schoolwork or class participation was required of me; I would wander about in a grayed-out haze of disassociation. Fortunately, the visits stopped by the time I was 9 or 10. 

Some of these details were known to me. Others were not. I've been a practicing ceremonial magician for the better part of a decade now, and some of the details that I did know suddenly make more sense in the light of occult philosophy. I never suspected this before, but it sounds like my father may have become possessed by a demon or other malevolent spirit. What do you think?

replies.content:
Hello Anonymous,

I walk a Christian path that includes the occult and polytheism. I'll offer my thoughts.

A) I'm a practicing Roman Catholic who has adopted JMG's theory about Christianity in his essay "The God From the House of Bread." I'm also a henotheist (meaning I only provide latria-worship to the Holy Trinity) who offers dulia to other beings, including Saints, Angels, Nature Spirits, and other Deities. I haven't encountered any difficulties as a Catholic doing this. The latria-dulia distinction in Catholic theology (as well as Orthodox theology) makes it fairly easy for me to square, to my conscience at least, the practice of honoring spiritual beings other than the Holy Trinity. One denomination that would provide even more flexibility would be the Episcopal Church, or some other liberal flavoring of Anglicanism. My dad was an Episcopalian and their worship services were always suitably uplifting, in a spiritual sense. I don't have a lot of experience with other Christian groups, so that's the extent of my ability to answer this question.

B) I don't see anything that I do as unethical. Like I said, Catholicism makes a distinction between worship that is "latria," which is reserved for the Holy Trinity, and worship that is "dulia," which is honor paid to creatures that, in some way, reflect the Holy Trinity. Saints and Angels fall under that. My innovation (if one may call it that) is to recognize that, since wisdom is a good and right thing, then honoring Minerva with dulia is also appropriate. Or since the spirits of Nature in my area reflect the glory of the Holy Trinity as a creative power that brings forth beauty and the natural world, then offering them dulia is also appropriate.

Personally, I don't feel this is insincere or misleading. My priest doesn't quiz me before receiving Holy Communion. There is also a lot of leeway in interpreting the Church's doctrines. That has been the case since the time of Vatican II in the 1960s. I try to stay within the boundaries set by various infallible teachings, and showing deference to non-infallible teachings. How successful I truly am at that might be debatable, but my conscience is satisfied.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You might also want to look into the independent sacramental movement. There are a lot of small churches that have apostolic succession and practice a fully sacramental Christianity but aren't stuck on the literal meaning of Christianity's rich symbolic narratives. The Liberal Catholic Church is one of these, and there are also a good many Gnostic churches that have a similar focus.

comments.title: Maximum nuisance
comments.content:
Is there anything magical behind what I would refer to as "the law of maximim nuisance"? It has several other names, such as Murphy's law and (if you'll permit the mild profanity) Sod's law, but basically follows that if something can be annoying or go wrong, it probably will.

Now there's a reasonable amount of perception bias behind this, as well as probably just plain entropy, but, for example, when I have been cycling for half an hour with not a single other person on the road, why does the only car show up from the other direction at exactly the point I need to move around a parked car or other obstacle...?

replies.title: Re: Maximum nuisance
replies.content: I don't know of a magical principle behind it, and always took it as evidence for the claim that Lovecraft was right and the universe is snickering at us behind our backs. "Sod," by the way, isn't a profanity in American English, so by all means use it when appropriate.

replies.title: Re: Maximum nuisance
replies.content: It's just plain old common garden variety probability. Because the most annoying/harmful/tragic outcome in any situation is part of the set of possible outcomes, they will crop up regularly because you're rolling the dice non stop as part of the course of life. The point of Murphy's law as an engineering principle is to design the worst outcomes out of the set of possible outcomes.

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer. I've recently been using Geomancy in conjunction with my attempts at investing, and I was wondering if you could clear up a few things.

-You've said that people who use magic to get rich with no effort wind up poor, but this doesn't apply to financial astrology since the information on the planets' influence on the markets is available to anybody. Does the same apply to Geomancy?

-I have been watching a promising crypto project and was waffling on investing. Unlike many other crypto coins that run on somebody else's network and whose only value comes from speculators, this project IS a network - a network for trading cryptocurrencies similar to a dozen existing trading websites - and owning a certain amount of the crypto coin used as a medium of exchange on the network is used as collateral for the network to allow you to set up a node on your home computer that will process network transactions and be rewarded a fraction of a percent of all those transactions.

I had been waffling for a while whether to invest enough to run a node, so I asked Geomancy "Will owning a [projectname] Node help me achieve financial independence the way I'm hoping?" I expected a no, but got this chart in return:
[IMAGE:0]
This appears to be a perfection by mutation through the 5th and 6th Houses.
So I immediately cast another chart for "Will [cryptocoin] go below $0.44 USD on [exchange I'm using] before next Friday?" and got this chart:
[IMAGE:1]
It looks like "no" right? A few moments later, the price fell by about 4%. I was baffled at how the oracle could be wrong, but with a bit of meditation later I realized that on a cryptocurrency exchange, one does not actually own the cryptocoins being traded, just IOUs from the exchange that you own a piece of the total pool of cryptocoin they hold (until you withdraw into your own digital wallet, bank account, or node software in my case). In your book on Geomancy you wrote that "The eighth house also governs...money or property the querent has loaned to another person." This would mean that the chart in fact perfects to the eighth house via conjunction, and a conjunction from the eighth to the first (which makes sense, since it's the exchange that determines the price, not me).

Is this a reasonable conclusion?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content: Yes, both of those are reasonable conclusions. Since you are providing a service to others by loaning computer time for processing transactions, you're not trying to get something for nothing, so you should be fine in terms of blowback. May I recommend, though, that you read Galbraith's The Great Crash 1929? Whatever the long-term value (or lack of it) of cryptocurrencies, there's a gargantuan speculative bubble building there, and you'll want to begin extracting money from the system sooner rather than later, stashing it in nonspeculative holdings, so you have something left when the bubble pops -- as it inevitably will.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:

I view crypto as a dotcom bubble - the tech is sound, there's lots of money to be made while its new and the niches are still being filled (if you can tell which projects are based on tech and which are vapourware), but once all the niches are filled half of the market crashes as they realize they won't be "the next big thing", it all just becomes another boring piece of life and begins to decline as institutions get their grubby managing fingers on it. Real wealth comes from things that directly support respirating life - such as fertile land, gardens, livestock, medicine, children and community - in my humble opinion.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy

2) May I offer another piece of helpful advice? Don't try to time the market. Isaac Newton thought he could time the South Sea Bubble, and he lost everything he'd invested. I am not as smart as Isaac Newton, and neither are you. Instead of waiting for your holdings to rise to some fixed figure -- and please note that land is soaring in price right now -- pull some out at regular intervals and stash it in some nonspeculative, non-dollar-denominated form. Then, when the bubble pops and real estate prices swoon, you'll be able to step in and pick up the parcel you want for pennies on the dollar.

comments.title: Mutual reception and judgement
comments.content:
I was really pleased to see the third instalment of the mundane astrology course drop this last Sunday morning and I spent a very pleasant couple of hours going through it for the first time. It raised a question about mutual reception in my mind. I get the impression that such a situation is rare, but however rare, given the span of time you could theoretically look at it must happen with some regularity. Even more unlikely is two mutual receptions in the same chart and even more so three. As far as I can see, there's a theoretical maximum of six!

Just taking the possibility of two, it leads to four charts in total. The original, the chart with one swap, the chart with the alternative swap and the chart with both swapped. Three mutuals doubles that number and so on.

In the past and perhaps in the future finding and dealing with multiple mutual reception situations must have been at the limits of what could be accomplished by hand alone - but if there was the possibility of seeing all the resulting charts arising from mutual receptions - would that be useful in extending the subject? I don't ask this casually, it's a real possibility.

Related to this is how you decide whether or not minor aspects are involved. In the inauguration chart there's a single 45 degree semisquare. I was surprised to discover (as far as I could tell) that there are three more, none at 45, but within the mundane orbs.

I wondered if there were any rules or criteria for deciding whether minor aspects were relevant, or is this a matter for experienced judgement?

Thanks for MM and the course, of course.

Mauve Oscillating Puppy

comments.title: Concerning Hermetica in general and Hermes Trismegistus in particular
comments.content:
Hello JMG,
What are your thoughts concerning where to place the writings of the Hermetica - do you understand the book to be loosely based on a historic Thoth/Hermes, or is it to be understood as only a pseudepigrapha? I ask the question because I am attempting to develop an understanding of Western Occult thinking based upon Neoplatonism and so do not wish to become distracted by works that are not in line with that way of thinking.

Thanks and regards

replies.title: Re: Concerning Hermetica in general and Hermes Trismegistus in particular
replies.content: The Corpus Hermeticum is pseudepigraphic in the strict sense -- it's the work of a school of Hellenistic Egyptian thinkers strongly influenced by Middle Platonism, and the treatises were assigned to a mythic founder in the usual way. By all means focus strictly on the main Neoplatonist writers for the time being, but once you have their teachings down, I'd encourage you to read the Hermetica as a way to get a sense of the intellectual milieu from which Neoplatonism emerged.

Dear JMG,

I had a timely encounter with a tree today, and wanted to ask for some feedback.

I don’t usually work with trees, though I like them and acknowledge them. I’ve had a few instances of ‘strong inclinations’ after touching a tree in greeting, with a gentle and open heart, which I then followed to good effect.

Today, however, I encountered something outside my norm, and I wondered if you had any advice or suggested reading.

A particularly emotional thing came to mind, and I got teary right there, which is not my norm when standing alongside a public street. I felt the tree pull back a little. And then I felt the inclination to pick up a bit of branch and take it with me. As is courteous, I spent a little more time there, and then expressed my appreciation when leaving.

However, I am left with some questions, as my specific expertise is not in tree lore.

3. What can I do build my skills in courteous communication? Usually this is for exercise or running errands. comments.title: cell salt protocol question
comments.content: I've been doing the cell salt protocol for my kids for about a year now, but it just occurred to me this weekend that my twins were born a month early, should I be using the their birthday for the protocol or what "should" have been their birthday? (I put should in quotes because twins almost never go full term, but nine months would have been a month after they were born)

replies.title: Re: cell salt protocol question
replies.content:
In that case, they are missing four months of cell salts, not three. Several participants who were born prematurely have found that it's good to do all four. You'll want to combine the protocols for the two dates -- never more than three tablets of each, but all the cell salts listed for both dates. So, for example, if they were born on March 25 and should have been born in April 25, you would start with the former:

0°-10° Aries: Kali Phos. 2, Nat. Sulph. 3, Kali Mur. 3, Calc. Fluor. 1

And add the latter:

0°-10° Taurus: Nat. Sulph. 2, Kali Mur. 3, Calc. Fluor. 3, Mag. Phos. 1

And get:

Kali Phos. 2, Nat. Sulph. 3, Kali Mur. 3, Calc. Fluor. 3, Mag. Phos. 1

replies.title: Re: cell salt protocol question
replies.content:
I was born 3 weeks premature, and I have found it (very) helpful to do the amount from my sign & degree, and then add the salts (and the amounts) from my due date sign / degree. So I’m taking five different salts in the max amount when all the necessary salts for both signs / degrees are added together.

Also, I did some divination when I started to see if I should just do the three (ish) days of the moon for my birth sign, or do the moon for the sign I’d have been born under for a full term delivery, or both. And it was quite clear that I needed to do the amount and salts for both times, but only during my actual birth sign moon.

This might change over time, and I might need to double check now that it’s been a year.

And your situation might differ.

But that was my experience fwiw.

comments.content: How do you know if one of the archetypes isn't balanced or integrated in oneself?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Check your pulse. ;-)

Seriously, under normal conditions you can count on reaching maturity with none of the archetypes integrated, or even activated. All through childhood, education, and early adulthood, under normal conditions, your attention is outward, toward the world of material experience -- and that's exactly as it should be. It's in the second part of life that the focus turns inward and the archetypes begin to surface, and then they're only integrated if you integrate them, one at a time, by your own inner work.

Conditions aren't always normal, of course. Children who grow up in psychologically difficult conditions sometimes have to turn their attention inward long before maturity, or they may choose to do so as an escape from a miserable outer life. That brings problems of its own, since the outer world also demands attention and sometimes it requires veering back and forth from outer to inner. But the same principle applies: the archetypes are balanced and integrated only when, and if, you do the work of balancing and integrating them.

comments.content:
JMG,

You've studied a number of occult paths. Is this similar to cross training in physical activities in that, say, learning to juggle might improve your table tennis serve, or learning to sculpt might improve you ability to paint? Or is just plain interest? I like playing games so learning the rules to a new game is always interesting to me, but learning one game doesn't help me play another any better.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: It's something else. What we have in occultism today are the scattered fragments of what was once a vast and detailed science of spirituality. To borrow a metaphor from the novelist John Crowley, it's as though a great and intricate machine of bronze and glass was shattered by a furious mob, wrenched apart, broken to bits, and then flung into the streets to be trampled on and washed into the gutters. Later, people who wanted to figure out what the machine was and how it worked started hunting for the fragments and trying to figure out how they fit together. The various occult traditions each have their own collections of fragments, and some of them have figured out how some of the subassemblies worked. Only by studying a variety of traditions can I work out how a few of those subassemblies fit together, and how these spare parts over here complete this partial mechanism over there...

comments.title: Primary & Secondary Emotions
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

You have written a number of times about recognizing primary and secondary emotions in the context of using journaling to unpack issues. This seems like a tool I want to pick up and use.

I have looked into it a little bit and many psychologists seem to define primary emotions as the emotional reaction to a cue, such as anger towards an insult, and a secondary reaction as an emotional reaction to a primary reaction. Is this the definition you are using, or is there a specific branch of occultism or psychology that you utilize?

I realize this is not an occultism question, so apologies for that. As a result of my SOP/meditation practice over the last three years, I have become slightly more aware of my emotional state and I can avoid automatic reactions more often than I could before. Processing emotions sure isn't always easy though!

replies.title: Re: Primary & Secondary Emotions
replies.content: The material on primary and secondary emotions I use is from the teachings of the Order of Spiritual Alchemy, one of the traditions in which I was initiated by John Gilbert. In that system, a secondary emotion is an emotion you embrace because it's less painful than some other emotion. For example, if you're overwhelmed by grief, fear, or shame, that's very painful, and so a lot of people in that situation get angry, because being angry is easier to deal with than being grieving, fearful, or ashamed. Then you can suppress the anger, and the usual result is depression. (Most of the chronically depressed people I've met are full of anger under the depression, and full of grief or fear or shame, usually from unprocessed childhood experiences, under the anger.)
